title: A Big Boss Battle at the Fed: Will Powell Stay or Go?
summary: Imagine if your school principal's term was ending, but instead of leaving, they decided to stay as a teacher — right next door to the new principal. That's kind of what's happening at the Federal Reserve, the big bank that helps control the U.S. economy. The current boss, Chair Jerome Powell, has a term that ends May 15. President Trump has picked Kevin Warsh to take over. But Powell might not leave the building! He has a separate job as a governor that lasts until 2028, and he's hinted he could stay. If he does, it would be the first time since 1948 that an ex-chair stuck around. Why does this matter? The Fed makes big decisions about interest rates — the cost of borrowing money. Right now, inflation (rising prices) is at 3.3%, partly because the Iran war made gas more expensive. The Fed usually keeps rates steady or raises them when inflation is high. On Wednesday, they'll likely keep rates at about 3.6%. If Powell stays, he and Warsh could disagree on what to do, like having "two Popes" in one church. Some people worry this could cause fights. But Powell has generally supported lowering rates, so he might not change things much. The drama got extra spicy because of a Justice Department investigation into Powell. A senator named Thom Tillis said he'd block Warsh's nomination unless the investigation was dropped. Last Friday, it was closed, but it could reopen. Tillis now supports Warsh, and the White House says Trump will be satisfied once Warsh is confirmed. Powell says he'll decide based on what's best for the country. So, stay tuned — this real-life power struggle could affect your family's wallet.
why_it_matters: The Fed’s choices about interest rates affect how much your family pays for a car loan or a mortgage, so who’s in charge can touch your everyday life.

keywords.explanation: The central bank of the United States that helps control the country’s economy.
When the prices of things like food and gas go up over time.

A top official at the Federal Reserve who helps make big money‑related decisions.

Whom has President Trump chosen to replace Powell as Chair?

Which senator said he would block Kevin Warsh’s nomination unless an investigation into Powell was dropped?
What happened with the Justice Department investigation last Friday?
options: It was moved to another state.

background_read: The Federal Reserve (often called “the Fed”) was created in 1913 after a series of bank panics to keep the U.S. economy safe and steady.
President Donald Trump (who returned to the White House in January 2025) wants Kevin Warsh, an economist and former Fed official, to become the next Chair.
If Powell stays on as a governor after his chair term ends, it would be the first time an ex‑Fed chair has stayed on the board since 1948, creating an unusual situation.
Article_Structure: WHO: Current Fed Chair Jerome Powell, incoming nominee Kevin Warsh, President Trump, and Senator Thom Tillis.
WHAT: A potential power struggle inside the Federal Reserve if Powell refuses to leave the building even after being replaced as Chair.
WHERE: Inside the Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States.
WHY: Powell has a separate job as a governor that lasts until 2028, and his presence could lead to clashes over interest rates — decisions that affect how much families pay for loans.

perspectives.description: Supporters say Powell’s experience as both a past Chair and an ongoing governor could keep the Fed steady during tricky economic times.
Critics worry that having an ex‑chair as a governor while a new Chair leads would be like “two Popes,” creating confusion and public disagreements.
This showdown is a test of whether the Federal Reserve can stay independent from the White House while managing political pressure.
